senses.definition: Characterized by a temperament or mood that is both sanguine (optimistic) and choleric (irritably irascible).
examples: The doctor's notes described him as sanguinocholeric, often cheerful yet quick to anger.
Her sanguinocholeric temperament made meetings unpredictable.

senses.definition: The state or quality of being sanguinolent; the presence or production of blood in a substance or wound.
examples: The medicine left sanguinolencies around the injured area.
There were noticeable sanguinolencies in the mixture after the cut.

senses.definition: The condition or state of being sanguine and bleeding; the presence of blood.
examples: Sanguinolency was observed in the experimental sample.
The report noted episodes of sanguinolency during the procedure.

senses.definition: Describing something as having or covered with blood; blood-stained.
examples: The knight appeared sanguinolent after the battle.
The painting showed a sanguinolent scene, dramatic and intense.

senses.definition: A device or instrument to measure blood-related properties or blood content.
examples: The researcher used a sanguinometer to assess blood levels in the sample.
There were no sanguinometer readings available during the trial.

senses.definition: Relating to the formation or production of blood or blood cells.
examples: Sanguinopoietic tissue stimulates red blood cell production.
The team studied sanguinopoietic pathways in the fetus.

senses.definition: Containing or discharging blood and pus; blood-tinged purulent exudate.
examples: The wound showed sanguinopurulent discharge.
Doctors noted sanguinopurulent material at the infection site.

senses.definition: Blood-tinged; containing or resembling blood.
examples: A sanguinous flow stained the tissue.
The soil appeared sanguinous after the storm.

senses.definition: The quality of being sanguine or blood-related; bloodiness or vigor.
examples: Her sanguinuity gave him courage.
The artist captured the sanguinuity of the scene.

senses.definition: A genus of plants in the rose family, known as burnets; some species have a reddish sap.
examples: Sanguisorba officinalis is used in traditional medicine.
The garden featured several species of sanguisorba.

senses.definition: The plant family that includes the genus Sanguisorba (burnets).
examples: Sanguisorbaceae comprises several herbaceous plants.
Taxonomists classified the specimen within Sanguisorbaceae.

senses.definition: A person or thing that drains or sucks blood; a bloodsucker.
examples: The myth spoke of a sanguisuge gathering energy at night.
In folklore, a sanguisuge could be found in dark corners.

senses.definition: Drawing or sucking blood; blood-sucking.
examples: A sanguisugent creature lurked in the shadows.
They warned of sanguisugent insects in the wetlands.

senses.definition: Bloodsucking; feeding on blood.
examples: The sanguisugous bat preyed on livestock at dusk.
Researchers studied the sanguisugous habits of the parasite.

senses.definition: Feeding on blood.
examples: Vampire legends describe sanguivorous beings.
Some specialized insects are sanguivorous.

senses.definition: Variant spelling of Sanhedrin; the assembly or council of Jewish elders in ancient times.
examples: The Sanhedrim met to decide on religious cases.
Historically, the Sanhedrin played a central role in Jewish governance.

senses.definition: The assembly of elders and leaders in Jewish law; the supreme religious body of ancient Israel.
examples: The Sanhedrin issued rulings on civil and religious matters.
Scholars study the decisions of the Sanhedrin in biblical times.

senses.definition: A member of the Sanhedrin; an adherent of its authority.
examples: The sanhedrist argued the law with strict interpretation.
As a sanhedrist, he valued tradition highly.

senses.definition: A collection or anthology in some South Asian languages; may refer to imagined or poetic works.
examples: The sanhita contains ancient verses and songs.
Students studied the sanhita of local poets.

senses.definition: A small flowering plant of the genus Sanicula, often called sanicle; also the common name for a medicinal herb.
examples: The garden included a patch of sanicle near the herbs.
Traditional remedies used sanicle for healing.

senses.definition: The flowers of the sanicle plant; the small flowering stalks.
examples: The sanicles added delicate detail to the woodland scene.
We gathered sanicles for the herb border.

senses.definition: An air-breathing larval stage of certain parasitic flatworms; a variant spelling of saniculate larva in some taxonomic contexts.
examples: The sanicula larva was identified under the microscope.
Researchers noted the saniculate stage in the parasite's life cycle.

senses.definition: A mineral form of potassium aluminum silicate, a tectosilicate of feldspar group.
examples: The rock sample contained sanidine crystals.
Geologists identify sanidine by its crystal structure.

senses.definition: Multiple crystals or forms of sanidine mineral occurences.
examples: The mineral tally included several sanidines.
Samples from the vein contained sanidines.

senses.definition: Relating to or containing sanidine; of or like sanidine.
examples: The sanidinic rock exhibited high-temperature minerals.
Scientists studied the sanidinic composition of the igneous body.
